# Don’t Write a Business Plan. Create a Business Model Instead

## Organized Notes



## What This Means for Your Work

**This video is the origin story of the tool that became the backbone of your entire training curriculum.** The Lean Canvas is not just a framework you teach -- it is the foundation of the SEED Initiative's approach to cooperative and social enterprise development. When you train cooperative leaders in Lean Canvas workshops, you are doing exactly what Ash advocates: replacing heavyweight business plans with lightweight, one-page models that get read and get updated.

**The "Zombie Startup" warning applies directly to cooperatives and social enterprises in BARMM.** Many cooperatives operate in markets that are too small or too local to sustain real growth. Your training curriculum should help cooperative leaders recognize this state and use the Lean Canvas to pivot or restructure.

**Action item: Update your Lean Canvas training module to include the 5-job value framework (Create, Deliver, Capture, Measure, Defend value) as a mental model before filling in canvas boxes.**

### How This Can Improve Your Claude Skills and Workflows

**The /lean-canvas skill should be updated to embed Ash's 5-job value framework as a pre-canvas diagnostic step.** Right now, /lean-canvas guides users through filling out canvas boxes. But Ash's insight is that before you touch the canvas, you need to understand the five jobs of a business model: Create, Deliver, Capture, Measure, and Defend value. Adding this as a structured prompt sequence before the canvas walkthrough would make /lean-canvas a more complete tool -- and would directly improve the quality of canvases produced during SEED Initiative workshops for Bangsamoro cooperatives and social enterprises.

**The /cooperative and /social-enterprise skills can use Ash's "Zombie Startup" diagnostic as a health-check module.** Many cooperatives in BARMM are technically alive but not growing -- they are the cooperative equivalent of zombie startups. Build a "Cooperative Health Check" prompt into /cooperative that asks three questions drawn from this video: Is the market large enough? Is the cooperative creating measurable value? Is there a path to sustainability without perpetual subsidies? This turns the skill from a formation tool into a strategic advisory tool, which is exactly what cooperative leaders need from MoroTech.

**The /training-assistant and /academy skills should incorporate the business plan vs. business model distinction as a foundational lesson.** When you deliver SEED Initiative modules to cooperative managers, many arrive expecting to write 60-page business plans because that is what banks and government agencies have historically required. Use this video's argument -- that business plans are static documents nobody reads while business models are living tools -- as the opening lesson. Encode this as a reusable training module template in /training-assistant so it can be deployed across all SEED cohorts.

**Cross-pollinate with /product and /enterprise-dev by treating MoroMarket itself as a business model, not just a product.** Ash's central argument is that the business model is the product. When you use /product to plan MoroMarket features, the skill should prompt you to validate the business model implications of each feature -- not just its user experience. Does this feature help Create value for cooperatives? Does it help Capture value for MoroTech? Wire /product and /lean-canvas together so every feature decision passes through the business model lens.

**Use /auto-research to systematically test which Lean Canvas box is the weakest across all SEED Initiative graduates.** Run /auto-research with the question: "Which Lean Canvas box do cooperative leaders struggle with most?" Survey past workshop participants, analyze their submitted canvases, and identify the pattern. That data feeds directly into /skill-optimizer to improve /lean-canvas and /cooperative, creating a Karpathy-style optimization loop for your training curriculum.
